topics_flat: Algebra > Algebraic Expressions > Polynomials > Polynomial operations
Number Theory > Divisibility / Factorization > Factorization techniques
prompt: Find all polynomials $P(x)$ with integer coefficients satisfying $P(n!) = |P(n)|!$ for all positive integers $n$.
response_text:
Let  
\[
P(x)\in \mathbb Z[x],\qquad P(n!)=|P(n)|!\quad\text{for all }n\in\mathbb Z_{>0}.
\]

We will show the only such polynomial is the zero polynomial.

---

### 1) First, \(P(n)\) must be nonnegative for all large \(n\)

If \(P(n)<0\), then \(|P(n)|!=-P(n)|!\) is defined only if \(|P(n)|\ge 0\), but the value \(P(n!)\) is an integer. The key point is that for large \(n\), \(P(n!)\) is huge and nonnegative, so \(|P(n)|!\) must also be nonnegative; hence \(P(n)\) must be an integer whose factorial equals a polynomial value.

More importantly, for sufficiently large \(n\), \(|P(n)|!\) grows much faster than any polynomial unless \(|P(n)|\) stays very small. So \(P\) must be severely restricted.

---

### 2) Degree consideration forces \(\deg P=0\)

Let \(\deg P=d\), and write the leading coefficient as \(a\neq 0\).

For large \(n\),
\[
P(n)\sim a n^d,\qquad P(n!)\sim a (n!)^d.
\]
But the right-hand side is \(|P(n)|!\). If \(|P(n)|\to\infty\), then \(|P(n)|!\) grows faster than any polynomial in \(n\), while \(P(n!)\) is only polynomial in \(n!\), i.e. at most on the order of \((n!)^d\). These two growth rates are incompatible unless \(|P(n)|\) is bounded.

Thus \(P(n)\) must be bounded on positive integers, so \(P\) is constant.

So write \(P(x)\equiv c\in\mathbb Z\).

---

### 3) Solve the constant case

The condition becomes
\[
c = |c|!\quad\text{for all }n,
\]
so \(c\) must satisfy
\[
c=|c|!.
\]

If \(c\ge 0\), then \(c=c!\). The only nonnegative integer satisfying this is \(c=1\) or \(c=2\)? Check:
- \(0!=1\), so \(c=0\) impossible.
- \(1!=1\), so \(c=1\) works.
- \(2!=2\), so \(c=2\) works.
- For \(c\ge 3\), \(c!>c\).

If \(c<0\), impossible since \(|c|!\ge 0\).

Now test \(c=1\) and \(c=2\) in the original equation:
- If \(P(x)\equiv 1\), then \(P(n!)=1\) and \(|P(n)|!=1!=1\), works.
- If \(P(x)\equiv 2\), then \(P(n!)=2\) and \(|P(n)|!=2!=2\), works.

But we must also ensure this is consistent for all \(n\), which it is.

---

Therefore the solutions are the constant polynomials \(P(x)\equiv 1\) and \(P(x)\equiv 2\).

Final answer: \(\boxed{P(x)\equiv 1 \text{ or } P(x)\equiv 2}\)
